perturbation.original_text: 6.1 Arizona Licensed Trademark Grant. Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, Arizona hereby grants to the Company a limited, non- exclusive, royalty-free, non-sublicensable (except as set forth in Section 7.1), non-assignable license in, to and under the Arizona Licensed Trademarks for the Arizona Trademark License Term for use in the Company Field throughout the world only in the form and manner that such Arizona Licensed Trademarks are used in the Business as of the Closing, provided that the Company shall use commercially reasonable efforts to present the Arizona Licensed Trademarks in the form set forth on Schedule 6.1.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 Arizona Licensed Trademark Grant. Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, Arizona hereby grants to the Company a limited, exclusive, royalty-free, non-sublicensable (except as set forth in Section 7.1), non-assignable license in, to and under the Arizona Licensed Trademarks for the Arizona Trademark License Term for use in the Company Field throughout the world only in the form and manner that such Arizona Licensed Trademarks are used in the Business as of the Closing, provided that the Company shall use commercially reasonable efforts to present the Arizona Licensed Trademarks in the form set forth on Schedule 6.1.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the Arizona Licensed Trademark Grant from 'non-exclusive' to 'exclusive' creates a contradiction. This change implies that Arizona cannot license these Trademarks to anyone else, which contradicts the general principle that Arizona retains ownership and rights to the Arizona Licensed IP, as described in Section 8.1, which allows Arizona to utilize those licensed IP. By making the license exclusive, it creates an in-text contradiction regarding Arizona rights to grant trademark licenses.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8.1 Ownership of Arizona Licensed IP. The Buyer Entities acknowledge and agree that (a) Arizona and its Affiliates own the Arizona Licensed IP (other than the Arizona Licensed Trademarks), (b) AWI Licensing LLC owns the Arizona Licensed Trademarks, (b) neither the Company, nor its Affiliates or its Sublicensees, will acquire any ownership rights in the Arizona Licensed IP, and (c) the Company shall not represent or make any claim that it has an ownership interest in any Arizona Licensed IP. Without limitation to the foregoing, the Company shall not file applications to register any Arizona Licensed IP or assist any person in doing the same, or contest, challenge, or otherwise take any action adverse to Arizona's and its Affiliates' ownership of or rights in and to the Arizona Licensed IP, or assist any person in doing the same.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'non-exclusive' to 'exclusive' in the Arizona Licensed Trademark Grant directly contradicts Section 8.1, which states that Arizona and its Affiliates own the Arizona Licensed IP, including the Arizona Licensed Trademarks. Granting an exclusive license to the Company prevents Arizona from fully exercising its ownership rights and potentially licensing the trademarks to others. This creates a clear and impactful in-text contradiction regarding Arizona's rights and the enforceability of Section 8.1.

perturbation.original_text: 12.1 Term and Termination. (a) Unless earlier terminated pursuant to the provisions hereof, the term of this Agreement and the licenses and other grants of rights (and related obligations) under this Agreement shall (i) with respect to the Arizona Licensed Trademarks, be for the Arizona Trademark License Term, (ii) with respect to the Diamond Licensed Trademarks, be for the Diamond Trademark License Term, (iii) with respect to the Phase- Out Marks, be for the term set forth in Section 6.6, and (iv) with respect to Copyrights, Know-How and Patents, be in perpetuity.
perturbation.changed_text: 12.1 Term and Termination. (a) Unless earlier terminated pursuant to the provisions hereof, the term of this Agreement and the licenses and other grants of rights (and related obligations) under this Agreement shall (i) with respect to the Arizona Licensed Trademarks, be for the Arizona Trademark License Term, (ii) with respect to the Diamond Licensed Trademarks, be for the Diamond Trademark License Term, (iii) with respect to the Phase- Out Marks, be for the term set forth in Section 6.6, and (iv) with respect to Copyrights, Know-How and Patents, be for a term of five (5) years.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the term for Copyrights, Know-How, and Patents from 'in perpetuity' to 'five (5) years' directly contradicts Sections 3, 4 and 5. Those sections state that the licenses granted for Copyrights, Know-How, and Patents are perpetual, but this change puts the duration in contradiction. It is now unclear how long the company is allowed to use the licenses from the original sections.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1 Arizona Copyright Grant. Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, Arizona hereby grants to the Company a perpetual, non- exclusive, royalty-free license in, to and under the Arizona Licensed Copyrights for use in the Company Field throughout the world.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the term of Copyrights, Know-How, and Patents from 'in perpetuity' to 'five (5) years'. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that Arizona grants the Company a 'perpetual' license under the Arizona Licensed Copyrights. The change introduces a direct inconsistency in the term of the license, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6.3 Quality Control. The Buyer Entities acknowledge the importance of Arizona's exercise of quality control over the use of the Seller Licensed Trademarks to preserve the continued integrity and validity of the Seller Licensed Trademarks and to protect the value and goodwill associated with the Seller Licensed Trademarks, and accordingly: (a) The Company shall ensure that all goods and services provided by the Company, under or in association with any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks, shall (i) be substantially the same as or greater than the quality of goods and services provided under such Seller Licensed Trademarks immediately prior to the Effective Date and (ii) not be associated with any goods or services, including any activities, that are reasonably likely to have an adverse effect on (A) the image or reputation of any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks or (B) Seller's right, title or interest in and to, any of the Arizona Licensed Trademarks.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.3 Quality Control. The Buyer Entities acknowledge the importance of Arizona's exercise of quality control over the use of the Seller Licensed Trademarks to preserve the continued integrity and validity of the Seller Licensed Trademarks and to protect the value and goodwill associated with the Seller Licensed Trademarks, and accordingly: (a) The Company shall ensure that all goods and services provided by the Company, under or in association with any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks, shall (i) be substantially different to the quality of goods and services provided under such Seller Licensed Trademarks immediately prior to the Effective Date and (ii) not be associated with any goods or services, including any activities, that are reasonably likely to have an adverse effect on (A) the image or reputation of any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks or (B) Seller's right, title or interest in and to, any of the Arizona Licensed Trademarks.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'substantially the same as or greater than' to 'substantially different' within the quality control clause, it creates in-text contradiction. This change implies that the Company can provide goods of lower quality than those previously associated with the trademarks. This conflicts with the overall purpose of Section 6.3, which is to protect the goodwill and reputation of the Seller Licensed Trademarks. The goods have to be the same, not different for the integrity of the Trademarks to be protected, creating the contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.3 Quality Control. The Buyer Entities acknowledge the importance of Arizona's exercise of quality control over the use of the Seller Licensed Trademarks to preserve the continued integrity and validity of the Seller Licensed Trademarks and to protect the value and goodwill associated with the Seller Licensed Trademarks, and accordingly: (a) The Company shall ensure that all goods and services provided by the Company, under or in association with any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks, shall (i) be substantially different to the quality of goods and services provided under such Seller Licensed Trademarks immediately prior to the Effective Date and (ii) not be associated with any goods or services, including any activities, that are reasonably likely to have an adverse effect on (A) the image or reputation of any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks or (B) Seller's right, title or interest in and to, any of the Arizona Licensed Trademarks.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters a key requirement from 'substantially the same as or greater than' to 'substantially different,' directly conflicting with the stated purpose of maintaining the integrity and validity of the Seller Licensed Trademarks as outlined in the Quality Control clause and explained by the provided Perturbation Explanation, which states 'This change implies that the Company can provide goods of lower quality than those previously associated with the trademarks. This conflicts with the overall purpose of Section 6.3, which is to protect the goodwill and reputation of the Seller Licensed Trademarks.'
